Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Nephrology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 30 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Nephrology.

30articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
American journal of kidney diseases : the official journal of the National Kidney Foundation

Risks of Hypocalcemia and Other Bone Mineral Disorders for Denosumab Versus Zoledronate Across the Spectrum of Kidney Function: A Target Trial Emulation

Xiao R, Faucon AL, He N et al. · 2026 Apr 18
Study Type: Observational cohort study with active comparator (target trial emulation)
Key Question: Does denosumab versus zoledronate carry different risks of hypocalcaemia and bone mineral disorders across varying levels of kidney function?
Key Findings:
  • Denosumab increased hypocalcaemia risk vs zoledronate (HR 2.10, 95% CI 1.28-4.33; absolute risk difference 0.72%), with events occurring mainly within first two weeks
  • Risk differences were substantially higher in patients with eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² compared to those with eGFR ≥60: hypocalcaemia (2.12% vs 0.19%) and secondary hyperparathyroidism (4.84% vs 0.03%)
  • Similar patterns observed for hypophosphataemia, though interaction with eGFR was less pronounced
Clinical Relevance: UK nephrology services should implement enhanced biochemical monitoring protocols for CKD patients receiving denosumab, particularly those with eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m², given substantially elevated risks of clinically significant bone mineral disorders.
Limitations: Observational design with potential residual confounding and limited generalisability beyond Swedish healthcare setting.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of kidney diseases : the official journal of the National Kidney Foundation

Cystatin C and Creatinine-Based Estimated GFR and Disease Activity Biomarkers in Rheumatoid Arthritis

Fukui S, Inker LA, Santacroce LM et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Secondary observational analysis of RCT data
Key Question: How do cystatin C-based and creatinine-based eGFR measurements respond to rheumatoid arthritis treatment and correlate with inflammatory biomarkers?
Key Findings:
  • eGFRcys was consistently 20.9 mL/min/1.73m² lower than eGFRcr at baseline (63.3 vs 84.2 mL/min/1.73m²)
  • Neither eGFR measure changed significantly over 24 weeks of RA treatment despite reductions in inflammatory markers
  • Multiple inflammatory biomarkers (VCAM-1, IL-6, TNF-RI, leptin, resistin) were inversely associated with baseline eGFR values, but only TNF-RI changes correlated with eGFRcys changes during follow-up
Clinical Relevance: This challenges the reliability of both eGFR equations in RA patients for monitoring kidney function, particularly relevant given the nephrotoxic potential of some DMARDs used in UK rheumatology practice.
Limitations: No measured GFR available to determine which eGFR equation provides more accurate kidney function assessment.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of kidney diseases : the official journal of the National Kidney Foundation

Transitioning from CKD to Kidney Failure: A Mixed Methods Study of Patient Perspectives

Urbanski M, Siminoff LA, Waterman A et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Mixed methods study (surveys and interviews)
Key Question: How do patients experience the transition from chronic kidney disease to kidney failure and dialysis initiation, regardless of their prior nephrology preparation?
Key Findings:
  • All 47 participants found the transition tumultuous regardless of preparation level (49% had some prior care, 28% minimal care, 23% no prior care)
  • 94% initiated dialysis as hospital inpatients and 98% started in-centre haemodialysis
  • Four key themes emerged: patients felt unprepared despite prior care, chaotic diagnosis communication, intense fear during first dialysis, and struggle to understand their new reality
Clinical Relevance: This highlights that current pre-dialysis preparation in nephrology services may be inadequate, suggesting need for improved patient-centred transition programmes to reduce trauma associated with dialysis initiation.
Limitations: Limited to one US metropolitan area and excluded non-English speakers, limiting generalisability to diverse UK nephrology populations.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of kidney diseases : the official journal of the National Kidney Foundation

Allocation of Kidney Allografts From Donors With Kidney Donor Profile Index <35% and the Impact of Kidney Donor Profile Index Revisions on Access to Transplantation for Children

Sonnenberg EM, Amaral S, Zhang S et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: How are high-quality kidneys (KDPI <35%) allocated between children and adults, and how will recent KDPI calculation revisions affect paediatric access to transplantation?
Key Findings:
  • 23.4% of KDPI <35% kidneys and 34.3% of "ideal paediatric-quality" kidneys were transplanted into adults with higher allocation priority than children, with nearly three-quarters being multiorgan recipients
  • The revised KDPI-8 calculation (excluding race and hepatitis C) increased the proportion of Black donors (15.3% vs 9.9%) and HCV-positive donors (11.1% vs 3.6%) meeting KDPI <35% criteria
  • The proportion of ideal paediatric-quality donors remained similar between calculations (32.7% vs 33.5%)
Clinical Relevance: This analysis highlights allocation disparities that may inform UK paediatric transplant policy discussions, particularly regarding prioritisation systems and organ sharing protocols for children with end-stage renal disease.
Limitations: The study extrapolated KDPI-8 characteristics from historical data rather than prospective implementation.
PubMed →DOI →
American journal of kidney diseases : the official journal of the National Kidney Foundation

KDOQI US Commentary on the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Antineutrophil Cytoplasmic Antibody (ANCA)-Associated Vasculitis

Almaani S, Ayoub I, Geetha D · 2026 May
Study Type: Commentary
Key Question: How should the KDIGO 2024 ANCA-associated vasculitis guidelines be interpreted and implemented in US clinical practice?
Key Findings:
  • KDOQI work group endorses the majority of KDIGO 2024 guideline statements for ANCA-associated vasculitis management
  • Commentary provides US-specific clarifications and implementation considerations for the international guidelines
  • Focus on practical application of KDIGO recommendations within the American healthcare system context
Clinical Relevance: While US-focused, this commentary may inform UK nephrology practice by highlighting practical implementation considerations for the KDIGO ANCA-vasculitis guidelines, particularly regarding treatment protocols and monitoring strategies.
Limitations: Commentary nature means no new primary data or evidence synthesis is provided beyond expert opinion on guideline implementation.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical journal of the American Society of Nephrology : CJASN

APOL1 Genetic Testing and Counseling Does Not Reduce Living Kidney Donors' Willingness to Donate: A Hybrid Type 2 Non-Randomized Controlled Trial

Gordon EJ, Gacki-Smith J, Smith JD et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Prospective non-randomized controlled trial (hybrid type 2 effectiveness-implementation study)
Key Question: Does APOL1 genetic testing and counseling reduce decisional conflict and willingness to donate among potential living kidney donors of African ancestry?
Key Findings:
  • No significant differences between intervention and control groups for decisional conflict (Beta -1.05, 95% CI -4.13 to 2.03, P=0.50), preparation for decision-making, or willingness to donate (Beta 0.18, 95% CI -0.29 to 0.66, P=0.45)
  • Intervention group reported significantly less satisfaction with the amount of information provided for decision-making (Beta -0.34, 95% CI -0.65 to -0.02, P=0.04)
  • 280 donors enrolled (71% participation rate), predominantly female (59%), mean age 40.2 years
Clinical Relevance: Demonstrates that APOL1 genetic testing can be safely integrated into donor evaluation protocols without deterring donation, addressing concerns about implementing genetic screening for high-risk donors of African ancestry in UK transplant programmes.
Limitations: Non-randomized design limits causal inference and may introduce selection bias between intervention and control groups.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical journal of the American Society of Nephrology : CJASN

Hospitalization Risk in the Integrated Home Dialysis Model: Analysis of the Canadian Organ Replacement Register

Desbiens LC, Tennankore KK, Goupil R et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does the integrated home dialysis model (PD followed by transition to HHD) carry different hospitalization risks compared to starting directly with HHD?
Key Findings:
  • Hospitalization rates peaked at 2.17/patient-year during PD-to-HHD transition (30% infection-related), with nearly 3-fold higher risk versus direct HHD (IRR 2.89, 95% CI 2.01-4.16)
  • PD-HHD patients had 38% higher hospitalization risk in the year before transition (IRR 1.38, 95% CI 1.03-1.85) but equivalent rates post-transition (IRR 1.04, 95% CI 0.80-1.36)
  • Shorter facility hemodialysis transitions (<90 days) showed less pronounced hospitalization risk increases
Clinical Relevance: This Canadian registry data informs UK nephrology teams about risks during integrated home dialysis transitions, supporting patient counselling and care planning during modality switches in home dialysis programmes.
Limitations: Retrospective registry analysis cannot establish causality and may have unmeasured confounding factors affecting hospitalization patterns.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical journal of the American Society of Nephrology : CJASN

A Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial of Ultrasound Evaluation of Fluid Tolerance for Acute Kidney Injury (USE-the-FORCE-for-AKI trial)

Gouin M, Huard K, Shen A et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Pilot randomized controlled trial
Key Question: Is it feasible to conduct a randomized trial comparing POCUS-guided fluid management versus usual care in patients with acute kidney injury?
Key Findings:
  • 100% protocol adherence achieved across 79 patients (40 intervention, 39 control)
  • POCUS reports changed clinical management in 50% of cases and were rated as useful by clinicians (median 4.5/5)
  • Intervention group had higher diuretic use on day 1 (40% vs 15%, p=0.01) but no difference in fluid balance, AKI progression, or clinical outcomes at 5 days
Clinical Relevance: Demonstrates that POCUS assessment of fluid tolerance (B-lines and VExUS scoring) is feasible and influences clinical decision-making in AKI management, supporting development of larger trials that could inform UK nephrology practice guidelines.
Limitations: Single-centre pilot study underpowered to detect clinically meaningful differences in patient outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
